title: Memory Management
summary: Understand Python's memory model, reference counting, garbage collection, and optimization techniques

sections.title: Python manages memory so you don't have to — mostly
sections.context: Python handles memory allocation and deallocation automatically, like a self-cleaning hotel room. Every object in Python has a reference count — the number of places that refer to it. When the count drops to zero, Python immediately reclaims that memory. This is reference counting, the primary memory management mechanism. A separate garbage collector handles circular references (object A references B, B references A, but nothing else references either) — these would never reach zero count. Python's gc module lets you interact with this collector: disable it, run it manually, or inspect unreachable objects.

sections.title: Reference counting and the gc module in action
sections.content: Observe how reference counts change and how gc detects cycles
sections.code:
import sys  # For sys.getrefcount to check reference counts
import gc  # For garbage collector inspection

def demonstrate_reference_count():
    # Create a simple list — its reference count is 1
    my_list = [1, 2, 3]  # ref count = 1 (the variable my_list)
    # getrefcount adds 1 because passing as argument also references it
    print(f"Ref count: {sys.getrefcount(my_list) - 1}")

    another_ref = my_list  # ref count = 2 (my_list + another_ref)
    print(f"After new ref: {sys.getrefcount(my_list) - 1}")

    del another_ref  # ref count drops back to 1
    print(f"After del: {sys.getrefcount(my_list) - 1}")

def create_cycle():
    # Create objects that reference each other (a circular reference)
    class Node:
        def __init__(self, name):
            self.name = name  # Node identifier
            self.ref = None  # Will point to another Node

    a = Node("A")  # Create first node
    b = Node("B")  # Create second node
    a.ref = b  # A references B
    b.ref = a  # B references A — circular reference!
    # Both have ref count 2 (variable + the other node's ref)
    # After del a; del b, ref count drops but not to zero — cycle remains

    del a  # Remove variable reference
    del b  # Remove variable reference
    # gc.collect() finds and collects the unreachable cycle
    print(f"Unreachable objects: {gc.collect()}")

demonstrate_reference_count()
# Expected output (approximate):
# Ref count: 1
# After new ref: 2
# After del: 1

sections.title: Memory optimization patterns for production code
sections.context: Python's convenient abstractions (lists of dicts, dynamic typing) consume more memory than lower-level languages. For memory-constrained environments, use __slots__ in classes to avoid per-instance __dict__ overhead — reduces memory by 40-60% per object. Use array.array or numpy for numeric collections instead of lists of Python integers (each Python int is ~28 bytes vs 4-8 for a C int). Use generators instead of lists for large sequences — they produce items one at a time without storing the entire collection. Profile before optimizing: use tracemalloc or memory_profiler to identify actual memory hotspots, since intuition about memory usage is often wrong.

sections.title: Circular references with __del__ cause memory leaks
sections.context: Defining __del__ (destructor) in classes with circular references creates uncollectable garbage. The gc module cannot collect objects with __del__ in a cycle because it doesn't know the safe order to call destructors. Wrong: class Node: def __del__(self): cleanup(). If cycles exist, these objects leak. Fix: avoid __del__ entirely; use context managers (with) for cleanup. Or call gc.garbage to inspect uncollectable objects.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Create a class with __slots__ defined and a regular class with the same attributes. Create 100,000 instances of each class and measure the memory usage difference using sys.getsizeof() or the psutil module. Also, create a list of 100,000 integers and compare its memory to a generator yielding the same sequence. Document the memory savings.
hints: How does __slots__ change the way attributes are stored on instances?
What's the difference between a list comprehension and a generator expression in terms of memory?
